# Claims Ledger

This file is the source of truth for every atomic claim made
anywhere on the open-source-ai-stack site. Each row is one
`(claim, source)` pair. Verdicts persist across audit cycles. The
ledger only grows; it is never re-extracted from scratch unless
prose has been substantially rewritten.

Schema definition lives in `audit/RUNBOOK.md`. Extraction prompt for
new rows lives in `audit/EXTRACTION_PROMPT.md`. Recall-pass prompt
lives in `audit/RECALL_PROMPT.md`. Verification prompt lives in
`audit/AGENT_VERIFICATION_PROMPT.md`.

## Verdict enum (full definitions in RUNBOOK.md)

- `supported` — entailed by cited source. Verifier confident.
- `contradicted` — contradicted by cited source. Fix the claim.
- `unsupported` — cited source does not entail. Add a better source.
- `consistent` — framing claim is consistent with sources (NOT verified).
- `pending_horizon` — prediction; resolves at horizon date.
- `source_unreachable` — source URL 4xx/5xx or snapshot missing.
- `verifier_unable` — verifier could not produce a verdict.
- `stale_pending_review` — source content drifted; re-verification queued.
- `needs_human` — verifier disagreement or reader-inference finding.
- `needs_verification` — new row, not yet verified.
- `discovered_uncovered` — recall pass surfaced a missed claim.
- `needs_source` — recall pass surfaced a claim with no cited source.

**Pre-push gate**: every row is `supported`, `consistent`, or
`pending_horizon`.

## Bootstrap status

The first extraction pass has not run yet. Run with:

```bash
npm run audit:extract
```

| layers.infrastructure.011 | layer-mdx | src/content/layers/infrastructure.mdx | Organizational decentralization (Akash, io.net, Bittensor) and physical decentralization (Crusoe flare-gas) are distinct shapes that are commonly conflated | framing | framing-prose | https://akash.network/; https://io.net/; https://www.crusoe.ai/ | consistent | 2026-05-19 | Framing fix that motivated this rewrite. Akash/io.net/Bittensor are token-settled marketplaces of independent operators (organizationally decentralized). Crusoe is a single proprietary company siting many physical facilities (physically decentralized only). The category distinction is real and the conflation is documented in the previous draft's probe_primer (which has been corrected). |
| layers.infrastructure.012 | layer-mdx | src/content/layers/infrastructure.mdx | Almost no artifact at the infrastructure layer is open-source in the OSI sense | framing | framing-prose | https://opensource.org/osd; https://www.oshwa.org/definition/ | consistent | 2026-05-19 | Editorial framing. Data centers are buildings; transformers and switchgear are commercial products; PPAs are private contracts. OSI defines open-source software; OSHWA defines open hardware but almost no production AI infrastructure meets it. |
| layers.infrastructure.013 | layer-mdx | src/content/layers/infrastructure.mdx | The central editorial tension at the infrastructure layer is concentration versus distribution of gigawatts | framing | framing-prose | (the site's own thesis) | consistent | 2026-05-19 | Editorial framing. Both sides represented in the body; consistent with the broader sovereignty-decentralization framing of the site. Layer-4 framing re-check applies. |

| glossary.quantization.001 | glossary-mdx | src/content/glossary/quantization.mdx | A 70B FP16 model is 140 GB; INT4 ~35 GB | factual | spec | https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.17323 | supported | 2026-05-19 | evidence: 70B params × 2 bytes (FP16) = 140 GB; 70B × 0.5 bytes (INT4) = 35 GB; arithmetic. |
| glossary.quantization.002 | glossary-mdx | src/content/glossary/quantization.mdx | FP8 is the data-center default on Hopper/Blackwell and MI300X | factual | spec | https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/nvlink/ | supported | 2026-05-19 | evidence: NVIDIA Hopper/Blackwell tensor cores natively support FP8; AMD MI300X also supports FP8 via ROCm. |
